beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with significant upstream production outside the Gulf; benefits directly from Brent spike as ~20% of global oil and ~20% of LNG transit Hormuz, and supply disruption lifts realized prices on non-Gulf barrels.
Same mechanism as XOM — diversified upstream with Permian/Kazakhstan/Australia exposure benefits from Brent premium without commensurate Gulf shipping risk.
Pure-play Permian E&P with no international exposure; full leverage to WTI spike from Hormuz closure with zero operational risk to Gulf assets.
Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; Hormuz closure cuts off Qatari LNG (~20% of global supply), forcing European/Asian buyers to bid up US cargoes. Direct spread beneficiary.

Scorpio Tankers — product tanker rates spike on rerouting around the Cape and tonne-mile demand explosion when Hormuz closes; war-risk premiums for vessels still transiting also lift charter rates fleetwide.
Frontline crude tanker fleet benefits from VLCC day rates surging on supply disruption and rerouting; historical Hormuz scares have doubled spot rates within days.

Lockheed makes THAAD, PAC-3 Patriot interceptors, and JASSM — direct demand pull from Gulf allies (Saudi/UAE) and US replenishment if kinetic conflict expands. Defense rotation typically lasts weeks on Iran escalations.
Raytheon makes Patriot, SM-3/SM-6, Tomahawk — direct beneficiary of Gulf air defense demand and US Navy munition expenditure if Strait conflict escalates.

losers.rationale: Delta has limited fuel hedging; jet fuel cracks spike with crude. Each $10 crude move historically costs majors several hundred million annually. Also exposure to international long-haul reroutes around Iranian airspace.
United has the largest international long-haul exposure of US carriers (heavy Asia/Europe/India routes that overfly the region); fuel cost spike plus potential airspace closures hit margins directly.
Carnival has bunker fuel as ~10% of opex with minimal hedging; oil spike compresses margins immediately and Gulf/Red Sea itinerary disruptions add cost.
Dow Chemical's ethylene/polyethylene cost structure tied to naphtha and natural gas liquids; Brent spike raises feedstock costs that cannot be immediately passed through in commodity chemicals.

Walmart's low-income consumer base is most sensitive to gasoline price spikes (gas as % of disposable income); historical pattern of discretionary trade-down and reduced trip frequency when pump prices spike >$0.50.
Counterintuitive but ~20% of Tesla revenue is China; oil shock + risk-off + China retaliation risk if US pulled into Iran conflict pressures multiple. Also high-beta name in any VIX spike.
Southwest's hedging program has been reduced; domestic-only network means no rerouting cost but full jet fuel exposure with no international yield offset.

primary_drivers: Crude oil supply shock — ~20% of global seaborne oil and LNG transits Hormuz
Shipping rate spike from rerouting and war-risk premiums
Defense spending and munitions replenishment expectations
Risk-off rotation: jet fuel/feedstock cost passthrough hits transports and chemicals; gasoline tax on consumer
macro_events.event_summary: Iran declares the Strait of Hormuz closed and fires on a transiting vessel, threatening ~20% of global oil and LNG seaborne flows.
headlines: Iran says Strait of Hormuz is closed again as vessel attempting to cross comes under gunfire

macro_events.event_summary: Iran war and $4 gasoline have sapped US consumer spending, with limited window to recover before lasting damage to discretionary demand.
primary_drivers: Higher gasoline prices act as a regressive tax on consumer discretionary wallet share
Geopolitical risk premium on crude oil from Iran conflict
Shift in consumer spending from discretionary to staples/energy

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with significant upstream exposure captures crude price premium from Iran conflict; refining margins also benefit from elevated gasoline crack spreads driving $4 pump prices.
Same mechanism as XOM — upstream leverage to elevated Brent/WTI plus downstream refining margin expansion from high retail gasoline prices.
Pure-play US refiner; $4 retail gasoline implies wide crack spreads that flow directly to refining EBITDA. Highest beta to the specific gasoline-price dynamic cited.
Trade-down beneficiary as pressured consumers shift discretionary dollars to low-price staples; historically gains share when gas prices squeeze household budgets.
Dollar stores over-index to low-income consumers hit hardest by $4 gas; same trade-down dynamic but even more direct exposure to the stressed cohort.
Active Iran war drives sustained defense spending expectations and munitions replenishment; direct revenue link to Middle East conflict tempo.

losers.rationale: Cruise lines are highly discretionary, fuel-intensive, and face double hit: consumer pullback plus bunker fuel cost inflation from Iran-driven crude spike.
Same dual-exposure as CCL — discretionary travel demand erosion plus marine fuel cost pressure on margins.
Airlines face jet fuel cost surge from oil spike while leisure travel demand weakens with stressed consumer; AAL most levered given weak balance sheet.
Casual dining (Olive Garden, LongHorn) sees traffic decline when gas prices absorb discretionary wallet; middle-income core customer is exactly the pinched cohort.
Discretionary mix (apparel, home) over-indexes vs WMT; consumer pullback disproportionately hits Target's higher-margin categories as shoppers defer non-essentials.
Gaming/leisure is textbook discretionary cut when consumers face gas-price squeeze and geopolitical anxiety; domestic Vegas traffic most at risk.
High gas prices accelerate mix shift away from ICE trucks/SUVs (Ford's profit center) while financing-sensitive auto demand weakens with stressed consumer.
Online travel booking volumes fall as consumers cancel discretionary trips; leisure segment exposure makes it more vulnerable than business-travel-heavy peers.

headlines: Iran war, $4 gas, took all the fun out of the U.S. consumer economy. There's only so much time to bring it back

beneficiaries.rationale: High-multiple growth name whose valuation is heavily dependent on terminal discount rate; a credibly dovish Fed chair directly supports expansion of its forward multiple.

losers.rationale: Charles Schwab earns substantial net interest revenue on client cash sweeps; aggressive rate cuts compress that spread directly. Also holds large AFS portfolio but duration gain is smaller than NII loss over multi-year horizon.

Moody's — a Fed perceived as politically captured raises the probability of a US sovereign credit review/downgrade and broader questioning of US institutional quality; reputational and regulatory overhang.

macro_events.event_summary: Reporting that Trump's presumptive Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh is expected to deliver rate cuts, with discussion of mechanisms he could use to do so.
primary_drivers: Lower discount rate / duration tailwind across long-duration assets
Fed independence risk premium — weaker USD, stronger gold and crypto as debasement hedges
Steeper yield curve and lower mortgage rates boosting rate-sensitive cyclicals (housing, small caps, regional banks)
headlines: Trump expects his Fed chair nominee to cut interest rates. Here’s how Kevin Warsh might try to do it.

macro_events.event_summary: Post-Iran war analysis piece argues the US has emerged as the world's most energy-secure power, implying durable domestic production advantage and reduced Middle East dependence.
primary_drivers: Structural premium on US-based energy production vs Middle East supply risk
Expected sustained capex in US shale/LNG infrastructure
Potential normalization of oil prices lower as US secures supply, pressuring high-cost/international producers

beneficiaries.rationale: Cheniere Energy is the largest US LNG exporter; thesis of US as dominant secure energy supplier directly translates to sustained European/Asian offtake demand and pricing power for US liquefaction capacity.
Diamondback Energy is a pure-play Permian Basin producer — the core asset base underpinning the 'US energy security' narrative. Benefits from framing of domestic shale as strategic national asset.
EOG Resources is a top-tier US onshore producer with no material international exposure; thesis-aligned pure-play on sustained US production dominance.
Venture Global operates Calcasieu Pass and Plaquemines LNG export terminals; direct beneficiary of thesis that US LNG becomes primary global supply-security hedge post-Iran conflict.
Phillips 66 refining footprint is US-heavy; benefits from stable domestic crude feedstock supply and potential widening of US-international crude price differentials.
Williams Companies operates the Transco pipeline — critical midstream infrastructure moving US gas to LNG export terminals; throughput grows with export-led energy security thesis.

losers.rationale: Exxon's large international/offshore portfolio (Guyana aside) and Middle East legacy exposure makes it relatively less pure-play on the 'US security premium' narrative vs domestic independents; also faces pressure if oil prices normalize lower post-conflict.

headlines: The real outcome of the Iran war: America is now the world’s most secure energy power
